skill.name: wp-performance
skill.description: Investigate WordPress performance with backend-only profiling, WP-CLI diagnostics, Query Monitor data, database checks, cache review, cron analysis, and safe verification steps.
skill.summary: Diagnose and improve WordPress backend performance using CLI-first tools and measured verification.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found the weak-crypto and most backtick matches are false positives from Markdown command examples and documentation links, while scripts/perf_inspect.mjs legitimately runs WP-CLI and reads WordPress drop-in files. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious network behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: WP-CLI Command Execution Requires Trusted Target
medium_findings.description: scripts/perf_inspect.mjs imports child_process and uses spawnSync to run the wp executable with user-supplied path and URL arguments. The command is executed without a shell, which reduces injection risk, but it can bootstrap the targeted WordPress installation and should only be used against trusted sites with operator approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct child_process usage and WP-CLI execution are present. The implementation passes arguments as an array to spawnSync, so command injection evidence is not present.

medium_findings.title: Local WordPress Filesystem Inspection
medium_findings.description: The helper script checks wp-content for object-cache.php, advanced-cache.php, Query Monitor, and Performance Lab files. This is legitimate diagnostic behavior, but it reveals local installation details and depends on a user-provided WordPress path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem checks are explicit and limited to expected WordPress performance files. I did not find evidence that file contents are read or exfiltrated.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged as Executable Patterns
low_findings.description: Most external command detections occur inside Markdown guidance for WP-CLI, curl, and diagnostic commands. These are user-facing examples, not code executed automatically by the skill, but users should run them only in approved environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown instructions and inline command examples. They are not part of an automatic execution path.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links and HTTP Examples
low_findings.description: Network findings are documentation links to WordPress, WP-CLI, Query Monitor, and example inspection commands. I did not find code that sends local data to an untrusted remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are URLs and a curl example for inspecting headers. No credential collection or outbound exfiltration logic was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static detections for weak cryptography map to prose and documentation text, not cryptographic code. No MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptographic operation was found in executable code during review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are natural-language text or documentation references. I did not find executable cryptography usage in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.spawnSync for WP-CLI Diagnostics
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses spawnSync to run wp commands. This is an intentional diagnostic capability, but marketplace users should understand that it executes local tooling in the target environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous primitive is present and semantically intentional. The absence of shell execution lowers the likelihood of command injection.

content.value_statement: Slow WordPress sites are hard to diagnose without clear backend measurements. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through CLI-first profiling, focused fixes, and repeatable verification.
actual_capabilities: Plans backend-only WordPress performance investigations without requiring browser access.
Runs a helper script that checks WP-CLI, WordPress version, doctor/profile availability, autoload size, and cache drop-ins.
Guides WP-CLI doctor and profile workflows for stages, hooks, cron, and targeted evaluation.
Explains how to use Query Monitor REST headers and envelopes for headless diagnostics.
Provides focused guidance for database queries, autoloaded options, object caching, HTTP calls, cron, and Server-Timing.
Emphasizes baseline measurements, production approval, and repeatable verification after changes.
limitations: Requires access to the WordPress filesystem, WP-CLI, logs, or authenticated REST requests for best results.
Does not provide browser-based visual inspection or frontend lab testing by itself.
Some recommended tools require installation or hosting support, such as WP-CLI packages, APMs, or Performance Lab.
Performance changes in production still require operator approval, backups, and staged rollout planning.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Slow Requests
use_cases.description: Find whether a slow page, REST route, or admin screen is dominated by bootstrap, queries, hooks, cron, or remote calls.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Production-Safe Optimization Plan
use_cases.description: Create a measured plan that avoids risky writes, cache flushes, plugin installs, and load tests without explicit approval.

use_cases.title: Review Platform Performance Signals
use_cases.description: Summarize WP-CLI doctor, profile, Query Monitor, object cache, and autoload findings into prioritized remediation work.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Performance Triage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-performance skill to triage a slow WordPress page. Ask for the target path, URL, environment, and write restrictions before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Run Backend Measurements
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-performance skill to create a backend-only measurement plan for this WordPress install. Include WP-CLI doctor, profile, curl timing, and repeatable baseline steps.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Specific Bottleneck
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-performance skill to investigate high database time and cache misses. Recommend safe checks for queries, autoloaded options, object cache behavior, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Production Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-performance skill to convert these profile findings into a production-safe remediation plan with risk notes, rollout steps, and before-and-after measurements.

output_examples.input: A logged-in admin screen has high TTFB on staging, and WP-CLI is available.
output: A baseline plan with the exact admin route, user state, and repeated timing samples.
A WP-CLI profile sequence for stages and hooks tied to that route.
A verification checklist comparing before and after results without changing unrelated settings.

output_examples.input: The site has many autoloaded options and no persistent object cache.
output: A ranked investigation path for autoload size, stale options, and repeated reads.
A caution to confirm ownership before deleting options or changing autoload behavior.
A cache rollout note that covers Redis or Memcached coordination and invalidation testing.

output_examples.input: Query Monitor is installed, but the agent cannot use the browser UI.
output: A headless REST request approach using authenticated responses and Query Monitor headers.
A summary structure for database, cache, hook, HTTP API, and PHP error signals.

best_practices: Capture a baseline before changing code, plugins, cache settings, or database data.
Run write operations only after explicit approval, especially in production and multisite environments.
Verify every fix with the same URL, user state, cache state, and measurement method.
anti_patterns: Do not flush caches, install plugins, or enable debug tooling in production without approval.
Do not compare performance results from different routes, users, environments, or cache states.
Do not delete options or alter autoload behavior without confirming ownership and runtime use.

faq.question: Does this skill require browser access?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for backend-only workflows using WP-CLI, logs, HTTP headers, and authenticated REST responses.

faq.question: Can it make production changes automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs the agent to request approval before writes, installs, cache flushes, or load tests.

faq.question: What tools work best with this skill?
faq.answer: WP-CLI, wp doctor, wp profile, Query Monitor, Server-Timing, logs, and platform APM tools provide the strongest signal.

faq.question: Can it inspect autoloaded options?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide WP-CLI checks for total autoload bytes and the largest autoloaded options.

faq.question: Is the helper script safe to run anywhere?
faq.answer: Run it only against trusted WordPress installs where you have approval. It executes WP-CLI and inspects selected local files.

faq.question: Does it replace load testing?
faq.answer: No. It helps diagnose backend bottlenecks and verify changes, but load testing still needs a controlled plan.
